auth.essence: Tesla reframed not as a carmaker but as the first embodied-AI company, the symbol of AI's shift from software to physical hardware.
auth.basic_md: Visser's highest-conviction embodied-AI long, explicitly made a new pick on 25 August 2025 around $340. The thesis: Tesla sits at the vanguard of AI's move from thinking to acting – robotaxis are the "ChatGPT moment" for intelligent machines, and Optimus extends that into humanoid labor. He insists you must stop modeling units and margins and instead price thinking embedded in a machine. The position has worked, returning roughly 45 percent off the call.
auth.basic_lg:
The idea began in March and April 2025 as Tesla was folded into a broad "embodied AI" hardware-frontier framework – every Tesla a mobile AI product, plus Optimus, vision-first autonomy, Dojo, and in-house chips/batteries as the vertical-integration edge. Through August 2025 it crystallized into a standalone bull case: Tesla as "the first embodied AI company," with FSD v14 and the Austin robotaxi pilot framed as the hinge where AI becomes the operating system of the physical economy.

In the most recent reports the framing widens rather than narrows. Late 2025 and 2026 pieces ("From Bits to Atoms," "The Physical World Upgrade") cite Musk's "AI is moving from thinking to acting" and position Tesla's camera-only autonomy and at-scale humanoid manufacturing inside the VLA (vision-language-action) and edge-inference shift, while noting cameras-alone forces the AI to solve depth computationally versus LiDAR. The robotaxi data flywheel and Optimus remain the core of the conviction.
tech_pts: (August 2025) Explicit new pick 25 August 2025 around $340; framed as the first embodied-AI company, not a carmaker.
(March 2025) Musk: Tesla to produce "several thousand" Optimus robots by end-2025; long-term Optimus revenue potential cited north of $10 trillion.
(August 2025) FSD v14 release seen as potentially shifting Tesla from carmaker to AI mobility platform if safety drivers can be removed.
(August 2025) Musk's claim FSD will be "abjectly humiliating" to skeptics within 12 to 18 months, maybe six.
(October 2025) FSD Computer cited alongside NVIDIA Drive Thor and Mobileye EyeQ; per-vehicle semiconductor content climbing from hundreds to thousands of dollars.
(October 2025) Optimus showcase expected at Tesla investor day November 6th; humanoids close the AI flywheel via vision/motion/task data feeding cloud training.
(December 2025) Tesla's camera-only approach forces computational depth-solving (brittle at night/fog) versus LiDAR's direct distance measurement.
(December 2025) VLAs need sub-10-millisecond decision loops, disqualifying cloud-only architectures and making edge inference a first-class constraint.
(September 2025) Robotaxi bet shifting to video-based training, framed as key to humanoid AI progress.
auth.tech_deep:
The mechanism is the data flywheel: vision-first autonomous driving and at-scale humanoid manufacturing generate vision, motion, and task success/failure data that flows back to cloud training, improving models that redeploy to every car and robot – turning trillion-dollar GPU investment into utilization at scale, not from chatbot queries but from millions of robots learning. Visser argues embodied AI requires the ability to scale from a manufacturing perspective, and that Tesla "seems like the only place it can come suddenly," given its vertical integration across in-house AI chips, Dojo, and battery tech (4680 cells). Robotaxis are positioned as the trigger event – the ChatGPT moment for intelligent machines.

The bull specifics center on FSD v14 and the Austin robotaxi pilot plus Texas regulatory momentum; removing safety drivers would reprice Tesla from carmaker to AI mobility platform, and success in vision-based autonomy directly accelerates humanoid (Optimus) deployment in factories and homes. Musk's Master Plans are read as a single integrated energy-and-intelligence system (cars, batteries, solar, autonomy, robotics), and Adam Jonas's evolution into a "hybrid auto analyst" is cited as the template for re-rating.

The key tension Visser flags is technical rather than valuation: Tesla's camera-only sensing forces the network to infer depth computationally, which is compute-intensive and brittle in edge cases like night and fog, whereas LiDAR offloads that burden – a debate left open. He also concedes current humanoid demos are "illusions of progress," machines that memorize rather than see, making genuine vision-first generalization the unproven crux. Catalysts cited include FSD v14, the November 6th investor-day Optimus showcase, and the broader 2026 adoption wave of digital and robotic employees.

pick.arc: The compute emblem → 'tokens-per-watt' moat → edge/Rubin transition
pick.note: His most-discussed name (26 notes). Conviction wobbled mid-2025 ('power is the new story') but stayed structurally long.

auth.essence: The compute emblem of the AI boom whose moat shifts from cloud GPU dominance to tokens-per-watt efficiency and edge-inference architecture.
auth.basic_md: Nvidia is Visser's most-discussed name and a high-conviction structural long, treated as the emblem of the AI compute era. The thesis is that Nvidia anchors every phase of the buildout, from training GPUs to the agentic-inference transition, even as the binding constraint moves to power and the bottleneck broadens beyond GPUs to memory, networking, and edge silicon. His conviction wobbled mid-2025 around the 'power is the new story' narrative but stayed long. Through the call it returned roughly 73.7% (from about 118 to 205).
auth.basic_lg:
The view began as the simplest expression of the AI trade: GPUs were the winner-takes-all bottleneck that drove Nvidia past a $3 trillion market cap, with H100/H200 chips the binding constraint. Over 2025 Visser repeatedly reframed Nvidia inside a wider system. The December Nvidia-Groq deal became his marker that Moore's Law had ended and value was shifting to architecture and memory-compute co-design, positioning Nvidia for edge inference rather than just cloud training.

In the most recent reports the emphasis is the Rubin transition and the physical-AI upgrade. Nvidia's roadmap (Blackwell ramping, Vera Rubin into 2026, Rubin Ultra 2027) is cited as validating the broader infrastructure case, while the constraint moves to power semiconductors, NAND/Flash, and the edge. Visser is careful that his enabling-layer calls (Marvell, Broadcom, TSMC) are 'not a call against Nvidia' but are validated by Nvidia's own pivot.
tech_pts: (March 2026) Nvidia reports over $1 trillion cumulative orders for Blackwell and upcoming Vera Rubin through 2027.
(March 2026) Vera Rubin targets up to 10x lower cost per token for agentic AI and MoE inference; production 2026, Rubin Ultra 2027.
(March 2026) Nvidia networking revenue rose 142% year over year in fiscal 2026, much of it Blackwell integration.
(February 2026) Vera Rubin GPU needs over 20TB high-speed SSD per unit, potentially absorbing ~20% of global NAND output.
(December 2025) Nvidia absorbed Groq's inference IP and talent (incl. Jonathan Ross, original Google TPU architect) via non-exclusive license.
(December 2025) Thesis: moat shifts from CUDA/HBM lock-in to memory-compute co-design (SRAM near compute) for edge.
(April 2026) NVIDIA's reported 800V DC power push in South Korea cited as edge/power-semiconductor signal.
(November 2025) H100/H200 scarcity drove Nvidia past $3 trillion; that GPU bottleneck has largely given way to grid constraints.
(November 2025) Lilly partnership: Nvidia compute and co-innovation lab power an 'AI drug factory.'
auth.tech_deep:
The core mechanism Visser tracks is the migration of the AI bottleneck. In the training era, transistor density and GPU supply were binding, giving Nvidia a winner-takes-all CUDA-plus-HBM monopoly that pushed its market cap above $3 trillion. His December 2025 Nvidia-Groq thesis argues Moore's Law has run out and the next frontier is architectural: moving memory closer to compute (SRAM) to cut data-movement cost in watts and milliseconds. By absorbing Groq's inference IP and Jonathan Ross (Google's original TPU architect), Nvidia signaled it intends to win edge inference, a market with different physics spanning 1.2 billion smartphones, 80 million vehicles, and tens of billions of IoT devices.

The forward bull case rests on the Rubin transition and order book. Nvidia reports over $1 trillion cumulative Blackwell/Vera Rubin orders through 2027; Rubin promises up to 10x lower cost per token for agentic and MoE inference, with third-generation NVLink and Inference Context Memory Storage. Networking revenue grew 142% year over year, and the platform's bill of materials cascades demand into adjacent scarcities: the Vera Rubin GPU's 20TB-plus SSD requirement could absorb roughly 20% of global NAND output, with a major producer demanding three years prepaid to secure supply.

The nuance, not a bear case, is that Visser increasingly frames Nvidia as one node in a broadening stack rather than the whole trade. The binding constraint moves to power (800V DC, BYOG, grid bottlenecks), to enabling silicon (Marvell, Broadcom for NVLink/custom), to CoWoS packaging at TSMC, and to the edge NPU layer. His conviction wobble in mid-2025 ('power is the new story') reflects this: Nvidia stays structurally long, but the incremental alpha he chases sits in the layers Nvidia's own roadmap validates.

auth.essence: AMD is a bullish secondary AI-compute name positioned for the inference and agentic-orchestration shift, where reasoning models and CPUs matter more.
auth.basic_md: AMD is a bullish, second-tier AI-compute pick beyond NVIDIA, gaining traction with its MI-series accelerators. The bull angle is the move from training to inference: Lisa Su's commentary that reasoning models make inferencing more important and increasingly distributed plays to AMD's strengths. In the 2026 agentic note it benefits from the broader idea that orchestration around the model runs on CPUs – 'the CPU has become cool again.'
auth.basic_lg:
AMD enters as a named beneficiary in the April 2025 embodied-AI hardware note – cited alongside Intel as a rival chipmaker gaining traction with its MI-series accelerators as the AI data-center buildout expands. The May 2025 inference note adds a direct earnings hook: Lisa Su arguing that new reasoning models make inferencing more important, with a shift toward distributed inferencing that 'plays to our strengths.'

By the March 2026 'Whole Rack' note the thesis broadens from GPUs to the full stack. As AI moves from chatbot to agentic, the orchestration layer – scheduling, state management, API coordination, workflow logic – runs largely on CPUs, captured by Intel's Zinsner saying 'the CPU has become cool again.' That structural shift expands the addressable compute opportunity in AMD's favor across both accelerators and CPUs.

(May 2025) Lisa Su: reasoning models make inferencing more important, with a shift to distributed inferencing that 'plays to our strengths'
(March 2026) Agentic shift moves value from GPUs to the full stack, including CPU-run orchestration
(March 2026) Intel CFO Zinsner: 'the CPU has become cool again,' demand picking up since H2 2025
(April 2025) Positioned in a buildout where hyperscaler capex could reach $300 billion in 2025
(April 2025) Grouped with Intel/Habana as accelerator competitors amid NPU emergence at the edge
tech_deep: AMD is framed as the leading non-NVIDIA way to own AI compute. In the April 2025 embodied-AI note it is highlighted as gaining traction with its MI-series accelerators, set against an unprecedented data-center buildout (combined Amazon/Microsoft/Google/Meta capex potentially reaching $300 billion in 2025). It sits alongside Intel and ASIC startups in a competitive accelerator landscape, with NPUs simultaneously emerging at the edge.

The core bull mechanism is the training-to-inference transition. In the May 2025 inference note, Lisa Su's Q1 2025 commentary is cited directly: reasoning models make inferencing more important and push toward distributed inferencing, which she argues plays to AMD's strengths. That positions AMD to benefit from inference scaling rather than only the training arms race.

The March 2026 'Whole Rack' note extends the case structurally. As AI shifts from chatbot to agentic, the system around the model – orchestration, scheduling, state, API coordination, security, monitoring – becomes as important as the model itself, and most of it runs on CPUs. Intel CFO David Zinsner's line that 'the CPU has become cool again,' with demand picking up in the second half of 2025, signals an orchestration tailwind that broadens AMD's opportunity across both accelerators and CPUs. The notes carry no explicit price target; AMD is a consistently bullish thematic compute name rather than a quantified single-stock call.

auth.essence: TSMC is "the factory that manufactures intelligence" – the foundry and advanced-packaging chokepoint whose CoWoS capacity is booked out through 2026.
auth.basic_md: A high-conviction long called 3 April 2025, framed as the indispensable toll booth of the AI buildout that evolved into a packaging-scarcity story. The binding constraint is no longer just wafers but CoWoS advanced packaging, which has become the chokepoint for AI-chip production. With capacity fully booked through 2026, Visser reads it as a hard signal that AI demand is structural, not speculative. The position has been the batch's biggest winner, up roughly 176 percent.
auth.basic_lg:
The call started in April 2025 within the broad embodied-AI hardware thesis, where TSMC headlined the AI-compute chip supply chain as the foundry that everyone – NVIDIA, AMD, custom ASIC designers – depends on. As the thesis matured through 2025, the emphasis sharpened from raw foundry capacity onto advanced packaging (CoWoS) as the specific bottleneck gating how many AI accelerators can actually ship.

In the most recent reports TSMC sits at the center of a "heterogeneous AI factory" and "AI factory transition" framing. The January 2026 piece quantifies the CoWoS ramp and the full-2026 booking; the March 2026 "OpenClaw" piece names TSMC as the manufacturing and packaging choke point in a live catalyst chain (alongside Broadcom, Marvell, Cadence, Synopsys), with a TSMC demand-validation event flagged for March 10.
tech_pts: (April 2025) Called 3 April 2025; high conviction; TSMC headlines the AI-compute foundry as the chokepoint every chip designer depends on.
(January 2026) CoWoS advanced packaging is the chokepoint for AI-chip production beyond NVIDIA.
(January 2026) TSMC expanding CoWoS from ~30,000 wafers/month to between 100,000 and 127,000 wafers/month by late 2026.
(January 2026) That CoWoS capacity is fully booked through 2026 – a hard signal demand is structural.
(March 2026) Named the manufacturing and packaging choke point in the heterogeneous AI factory thesis.
(March 2026) TSMC flagged to "validate demand again on March 10" within a live catalyst chain (Broadcom/Marvell earnings, Synopsys, NVIDIA GTC).
(March 2026) Thesis explicitly not a call against NVIDIA; Rubin and platform strategy validate the enabling-layer view.
auth.tech_deep:
The core mechanism: AI accelerators are gated not by transistor density alone but by CoWoS advanced packaging, which co-locates high-bandwidth memory and compute. TSMC is the dominant supplier of that packaging, making it the structural chokepoint of the AI chip supply chain. Visser's quantified evidence is the CoWoS ramp from roughly 30,000 wafers per month toward 100,000 to 127,000 by late 2026, with that entire expanded capacity already fully booked through 2026 – which he reads as proof demand is structural rather than speculative froth.

The most recent framing places TSMC inside a "heterogeneous AI factory" where the demand accelerant is agentic AI, and where the value accrues to the enabling layer: Broadcom and Marvell for custom silicon, TSMC for manufacturing and packaging, Cadence and Synopsys for the rising design-complexity tax. Visser stresses this is actionable now, not a distant secular story, pointing to a concrete catalyst chain – Broadcom/Marvell already moving the earnings math, TSMC validating demand March 10, Synopsys framing the engineering bottleneck March 11–12, and NVIDIA at GTC March 16–19. He is explicit the thesis is not bearish NVIDIA; NVIDIA's own Rubin and platform strategy validate owning the picks-and-shovels packaging chokepoint.

auth.essence: ASML is a recurring high-conviction chokepoint long – its EUV monopoly is the unbreakable bottleneck and toll-booth on every advanced chip the AI buildout requires.
auth.basic_md: ASML is a high-conviction long built on its EUV lithography monopoly, framed as an 'unbreakable bottleneck' and toll-booth on advanced-node fabrication. As AI compute demand scales, ASML's tools are the indispensable gate every leading-edge chip must pass through, pulling demand forward alongside DRAM and test/tool peers. The call (dated April 2025) has returned roughly 195%, anchor ~650 to ~1,920. It sits squarely in the AI Compute & Semis theme as one of the cleanest ways to own the physical bottleneck.
auth.basic_lg:
The thesis originates in the April 2025 'embodied AI / next hardware frontier' report, which placed ASML within the broader semiconductor supply chain benefiting from an unprecedented AI data-center and fab buildout – hyperscaler capex potentially reaching $300 billion in 2025 and data-center power demand projected to rise sharply through 2030. ASML's EUV role makes it a structural enabler of the advanced nodes that AI accelerators require.

By September 2025 the chokepoint framing sharpens: ASML (EUV) is named the key bottleneck for advanced chips amid widespread shortages across power and compute (DRAM, HDDs, transformers, turbines, cooling), with EUV-driven tool demand pulled forward as testing and tools surge alongside Teradyne. The October 2025 NPU/edge piece keeps ASML in the frame as the heterogeneous-inference and on-device era expands the universe of advanced silicon that must flow through its lithography.

Return ~195% (anchor ~650 to current ~1,920) per pick arc

(September 2025) Demand pulled forward as testing/tools surge, alongside Teradyne

auth.tech_deep:
The core mechanism is monopoly positioning: ASML is the sole supplier of EUV lithography, the indispensable step for advanced-node logic and increasingly for memory scaling. Visser frames this as a toll-booth – every leading-edge wafer the AI buildout demands pays ASML, making it a chokepoint long with high conviction since April 2025. The bull specifics are demand-side: hyperscaler capex potentially near $300 billion in 2025, data-center power demand rising steeply through 2030, and AI's spread to edge/NPU devices all widening the pool of advanced silicon that must pass through EUV.

The September 2025 report embeds ASML in a system-wide scarcity story – bottlenecks across DRAM, HDDs, transformers, turbines and cooling, with power availability the #1 siting constraint – where EUV is the chip-side equivalent. Second-order demand (testing, tools) is being pulled forward, with Teradyne and ASML cited as beneficiaries. The pick has already delivered ~195% from anchor, validating the chokepoint thesis; the implicit risk – not detailed in these passages – is the cyclicality and capex-sensitivity of the broader semicap complex it sits within.

auth.essence: Visser's single best call – long Micron since spring 2025 on structural HBM/DRAM scarcity, riding it ~+1,400% from $69 to four figures before it became consensus.
auth.basic_md: Micron is Visser's standout winner, a high-conviction long initiated in April 2025 on the thesis that AI compute would create a structural shortage in High-Bandwidth Memory and DRAM. As one of only three primary HBM producers (alongside SK Hynix and Samsung), Micron sat inside an oligopoly with pricing power as AI accelerators consumed ever more memory bandwidth. The stock ran roughly +1,400% from a ~$69 anchor to over $1,000, becoming the textbook example of his 'scarcity-driven hardware winner' framework. By May 2026 he flags that the trade has become consensus.
auth.basic_lg:
The call originated in the April 2025 'From Cloud LLMs to Embodied AI' piece, where Visser laid out HBM as the critical bottleneck feeding AI compute – stacked DRAM located close to the processor, with bandwidth on the order of hundreds of GB/s, essential as models grew. He framed Micron, SK Hynix and Samsung as an oligopoly all ramping HBM and fast DDR5, citing forecasts that HBM could exceed 20% of DRAM market value starting in 2024. This positioned Micron as a pure beneficiary of AI's appetite for memory.

By January 2026 Visser cited Micron's 'parabolic chart' as the archetypal scarcity-driven hardware winner, contrasting it against software's abundance-era drawdowns. auth.tech_deep:
The mechanism is supply scarcity inside an oligopoly. HBM is a specialized, stacked form of DRAM positioned close to the AI accelerator to deliver the memory bandwidth (hundreds of GB/s per chip) that training large models requires. Because only three firms – Micron, SK Hynix and Samsung – produce it at scale, surging AI demand translated directly into pricing power and earnings upside rather than being competed away. Visser repeatedly framed memory as the bottleneck feeding 'the beast of AI compute,' with HBM projected to exceed 20% of DRAM market value from 2024.

The bull specifics compounded over time: record/unprecedented demand reported by SK Hynix, second-order pull-through to testing and tools names (Teradyne, ASML), and the September 2025 Jensen Huang thesis that inference demand could scale 100–1000×, making the memory shortage structural rather than cyclical. By January 2026 Micron's parabolic chart had become Visser's reference example of a scarcity-driven hardware winner, explicitly contrasted with the abundance-era drawdowns of software (Salesforce, Adobe, ServiceNow).

The principal risk is the one Visser himself flags by May 2026: the call is now consensus after a ~+1,400% move from ~$69 to over $1,000. His evolving 'Strait of Hormuz' framing – that real demand becoming so strong is now the real risk – implicitly cautions that the easy phase of the scarcity trade is behind it, even as the underlying structural demand remains intact.

The view took shape in early-to-mid 2025 as Visser mapped the move from cloud LLMs to embodied AI. He highlighted Qualcomm's Snapdragon platform embedding NPUs for on-device features, alongside the projection that about 30% of new smartphones would ship with on-board generative AI by 2025. In the May 2025 'Inference Inflection' he cited Qualcomm's CEO emphasizing rapid edge-AI inference growth in automotive and IoT, enabling real-time processing for a multi-billion-dollar market.

The October 2025 NPU piece is where the thesis sharpens: Qualcomm is grouped with NVIDIA, Microsoft, Apple, and Tesla as platform winners controlling edge AI, the layer that makes cloud GPU infrastructure profitable through constant retraining demand. auth.tech_deep:
Qualcomm sits at the center of Visser's edge-inference architecture. The mechanism is the bifurcation of AI workloads: cloud training and coordination stay GPU-heavy, but execution migrates to the edge, where NPUs let a phone transcribe offline, a PC summarize without internet, and a car recognize obstacles before a network ping completes. Qualcomm's Snapdragon NPUs are his canonical example of on-device AI silicon, and he cites management pointing to automotive and IoT as the fastest-growing edge inference markets.

The bull framing is economic, not just product. In the October 2025 NPU report, Visser argues each NPU is a capital good producing cognitive work and each inference is a 'yield event' converting sunk cost into output, making edge silicon a compounding asset rather than static capacity. He groups Qualcomm with the platform winners that control edge AI through silicon IP and therefore gain pricing power and margin expansion, and ties them to the 'utilization flywheel' where edge inference generates behavioral data that retrains cloud models, in turn making GPU infrastructure profitable.

The risk is symmetric and stated plainly: if NPUs do not scale, AI remains cloud-constrained, too expensive and too slow, and the infrastructure buildout becomes a stranded asset with margins compressing. The medium conviction reflects that Qualcomm is a derivative bet on the edge thesis materializing, with competition from Apple's Neural Engine, AMD, Intel/Habana, and ASIC startups also chasing the same on-device and embodied-AI opportunity.

In the most recent reports the position is a washed-out laggard rather than a broken thesis. By June 2026 it sits near the 200-week moving average after roughly a 21% drawdown from the April 2025 call, and Visser reframes that as an accumulation opportunity. The latest notes keep Bitcoin inside the agentic-economy frame – the 'financial guardrails' where agentic intelligence leaves the chat box – alongside the fiscal trap that he argues forces the Fed's hand toward easier policy.

tech_deep: The bull case rests on two reinforcing pillars. First, fiscal dominance: in the March 2026 'D.O.G.E. 2.0' note Visser argues the Fed faces a structural trap across Debt, Oil, Growth, and Employment. Unlike the 1970s, today's economy is financialized – stock-market-cap-to-GDP exceeds 200% (versus ~42% in 1975 and ~38% in 1979) and debt sits at 122% of GDP – so a genuine inflation fight would force intolerable asset deflation into a weak labor market. The conclusion: every easing cycle comes sooner and every debt-heavy fight is shorter, and 'Bitcoin does not need hyperinflation for this thesis to matter.' Oil's surge from the Iran war and Strait of Hormuz disruption supplies inflation the Fed cannot directly repair, sharpening the bind.

Second, the AI pillar: Bitcoin is the 'purest AI trade,' the native money for an agentic economy where AI leaves the chat box and needs financial rails. This sits alongside Visser's stablecoin view (Genius Act framework, Ethereum as settlement layer, tokenization) as the financial guardrails of the machine economy. A distinct operational angle: Bitcoin miners function as 'virtual batteries,' monetizing off-peak power and curtailing during spikes to improve renewable economics and grid stability (ERCOT cited) – linking BTC to the AI power-bottleneck theme.

Across 2025 the framing layered digital-gold and dollar-debasement arguments (capital controls, remittance taxes, Asian repatriation of dollar reserves, a 'Liz Truss moment' in Treasuries) onto the AI thesis. Catalysts cited include strategic reserves, tokenization pilots, and 401(k) access driving large-scale inflows. The honest counterpoint is performance: from the April 2025 call near $82,000 the position fell to roughly $64,940 by mid-2026 (about -21%), making it a clear laggard in this window. Visser never wavered, instead reframing the drawdown – washed out near the 200-week moving average – as an accumulation opportunity rather than a thesis break.

auth.basic_md: Microsoft appears mainly as the textbook case for the AI capex flywheel and as a designated platform winner. In the May 2026 note Visser calls it 'the cleanest example' of a self-reinforcing loop: rising capex lifts supplier revenue today while the cost depreciates over years, RPOs signal future demand, and improving tax treatment helps the cash-flow math. It is also named a winner in software monetization and in the edge/NPU buildout, with Nadella's 'agents are the new seats' line used to frame AI as a substitute for wages.
auth.basic_lg:
Microsoft threads through the AI-infrastructure reports as both bull exemplar and concentration risk. In the inference and NPU pieces it is listed among the platform winners – Azure as an integrated cloud-edge strategy, software monetization at the application layer – that make GPU infrastructure profitable via constant retraining demand. In the September 2025 'Bubbles Lift Everyone' note it appears on the other side: one of a handful of mega-caps (with NVIDIA, Apple, Alphabet, Amazon) accounting for the bulk of equity performance, illustrating dangerous concentration.

The most developed treatment is the May 2026 'AI's Strait of Hormuz' note, where Microsoft anchors the demand-bubble debate. The argument is that the spending loop is rational and self-reinforcing – capex up, supplier revenue up now, depreciation spread over time, RPOs validating future demand, tax treatment improving cash flow, and a strategic race to secure frontier capacity – so the real risk is not a demand bubble but that hyperscalers, enterprises, sovereigns, and labs all treat AI capacity as strategically essential. Nadella's commentary ('coding is a substitute for wages,' 'agents are the new seats') is cited to support the broader thesis that AI is a new form of liquidity replacing headcount.

tech_deep: Microsoft's central role is as the proof-of-concept for Visser's defense of hyperscaler spending. In the May 2026 note he lays out the mechanism explicitly: cash leaves today but the expense is depreciated over years, so rising capex coincides with rising cloud revenue, rising remaining performance obligations (RPOs) that show future demand, and improving cash-flow math if tax policy accelerates deductions. The result is a self-reinforcing loop where supplier revenue, demand visibility, and the race to secure frontier AI capacity all compound – which is why he argues the simple 'demand-bubble' argument misses the bigger risk.

On positioning, Microsoft is repeatedly named a platform winner. In the NPU/edge thesis it appears for software monetization and as an integrated cloud-edge operator (Azure) in the 'utilization flywheel,' where edge NPUs offload inference cost and feed behavioral data back to cloud GPUs for retraining – making otherwise stranded infrastructure profitable. Nadella's quotes anchor the labor-displacement framing: AI as 'intelligence liquidity' that lowers the cost of work the way QE lowered the cost of money.

The counterweight is concentration. The September 2025 'Bubbles Lift Everyone' note places Microsoft among the narrow set of mega-caps accounting for most equity-market performance and corporate investment – the structural fragility beneath an AI-led tape. No price target or explicit trade is given; the stock functions as the canonical illustration of the capex-and-platform thesis rather than a standalone pick.

The mechanism is demand-side: real-time generative AI applications drive Cloud inference workloads, which Pichai cited as scaling rapidly; the 50x year-over-year jump in Google LLM token usage is the cleanest quantitative signal of that compute-and-power demand. By 2026 the narrative matures – the market 'knows the language' of agentic AI, so upside must come from execution, not mere recognition, raising the bar for the cohort Alphabet belongs to.

There is no discrete pick arc or conviction rating for Amazon in this set – it carries no entry, no return, and no stance stronger than 'mention' (with one 'bullish' inference-piece appearance). auth.essence: Brazil (EWZ) is Visser's macro expression of the embodied-AI era: a green-compute, weak-dollar, and rare-earth beneficiary made a new pick 3 June 2025.
auth.basic_md: A medium-conviction long picked 3 June 2025, expressing his regime-shift macro view through Brazil rather than a single stock. Three secular tailwinds drive it: structural dollar weakness, the AI hard-asset/commodity supercycle (clean energy as an AI-compute location advantage), and the reordering of global trade and capital flows. Brazil's rare-earth minerals, commodities, and clean power position it at the epicenter. The trade has returned roughly 22 percent.
auth.basic_lg:
The idea was seeded in the 1 June 2025 video, where Visser flagged Brazil as a beneficiary of returning tariff uncertainty, capital-control fears, rare-earth mineral demand, and a weaker dollar. He formalized it on 3 June 2025 as a new pick, drawing on his time living in Brazil and his habit of getting long when it is off investor radar – combining an embodied-AI commodity-needs angle with the now-real dollar-weakness and Asia-repatriation story.

The most recent (and only) detailed report lays out the full setup: dollar-weakness leverage to BRL and MSCI Brazil, an AI-driven hard-asset supercycle, and Brazil's pivot position between China (its largest trading partner) and US support for rare-earth processing. It pairs this with technical reads – USDBRL breaking below its 40-week average targeting 4.60, EWZ breaking out, IBOV's long-term cup-and-handle, and an Elliott Wave call that a 13-year BRL bear cycle is complete.
tech_pts: (June 2025) New pick 3 June 2025; medium conviction; Brazil framed as the trade of the embodied-AI era.
(June 2025) A 1% DXY drop historically yields 2.5–3.0% appreciation in high-yield EM currencies like BRL.
(June 2025) Over 22 years, each 1% DXY drop has lifted MSCI Brazil over 4%.
(June 2025) Brazil's gross public debt expected to reach 92% of GDP, a concern but not new.
(June 2025) China is Brazil's largest trade partner (~25–30% of exports); 2024 surplus with China ~$50–60 billion.
(June 2025) US Export-Import Bank supporting Brazilian rare-earth processing; BYD plant and lithium/EV investment from China.
(June 2025) Technicals: USDBRL broke below 40-week average targeting 4.60; EWZ reversed a false breakdown into breakout; IBOV cup-and-handle targets 154,000 and 174,000.
(June 2025) Elliott Wave read: a 13-year BRL bear cycle appears complete with a textbook 5-wave decline; tactical bullish.
auth.tech_deep:
The thesis rests on three secular tailwinds. First, structural dollar weakness: Visser cites the historical sensitivity where a 1 percent DXY drop translates to 2.5–3.0 percent BRL appreciation and, over 22 years, a 4 percent-plus rise in MSCI Brazil, with dollar-weakness periods historically aiding Brazil's fiscal repair via capital inflows, compressed imported inflation, and better debt affordability. Second, an AI hard-asset supercycle: the shift from a software era to a capital- and energy-intensive hardware phase (inference, humanoids, AI-native devices, full self-driving, power-hungry data centers) drives demand for the commodities, minerals, and clean energy Brazil supplies – clean power as an AI-compute location advantage. Third, global trade reordering and Asia repatriation of dollar assets.

Brazil's geopolitical positioning is a key support: China is its largest trading partner at roughly 25–30 percent of exports with a 2024 surplus of $50–60 billion, while the US (via the Export-Import Bank) backs Brazilian rare-earth processing – letting Brazil benefit from both blocs, including BYD's plant and Chinese lithium/EV investment. The acknowledged risk is fiscal sustainability, with gross public debt heading to 92 percent of GDP, which Visser dismisses as not new. The trade is corroborated technically: USDBRL breaking below its 40-week average toward 4.60, EWZ reversing a false breakdown into breakout, an IBOV cup-and-handle targeting 154,000 then 174,000, and an Elliott Wave read that a 13-year BRL bear cycle has completed via a textbook five-wave decline, with confirmation on a break above 0.1850. Expression vehicles span FX (long BRL/USD), equities (EWZ, banks, utilities, infrastructure), and local-currency rates.

auth.tech_deep:
Oracle's investment case is the cleanest data point in Visser's inference thesis. The September 2025 print showed roughly $317 billion in new RPO orders quarter over quarter, up 359% year over year, lifting backlog toward $500 billion, a figure he dramatized as equal to about 81% of US quarterly GDP. His read: this was not future hype but businesses already committing billions to use AI, and the demand was inference-driven, which is why he argued the implication runs through power, grid, and compute supply chains rather than Oracle alone. He explicitly told readers to 'forget what you think about Larry Ellison' and focus on the two messages, demand is massive and outstripping supply, and inference is the driver.

The bear points Visser acknowledges are the same ones the market fixated on: the RPO quality, margin pressure, and the debt Oracle may need to fund capacity it has not yet built. His counter was behavioral, that skepticism persisted 'even in the face of insanely large evidence,' and he warned the opportunity could be priced in before disbelief gives way. In hindsight the timing was the flaw: the loud reiteration landed right after a ~36% spike, and the stock fell ~9.3% from the call.

The later reframing matters for the forward view. By November 2025 (CoreWeave's warning), Visser argued the binding constraint had moved from capital to 'concrete', third-party developers failing to deliver power shells on schedule, signaling a regime change where build-out speed, not demand, gates returns. By April 2026 he placed Oracle's October drawdown inside peak-bubble fears that re-rated all software, and suggested Oracle is better understood as an AI-compute-shortage beneficiary than a disrupted software name, one to watch if software has marked a tradable low.

auth.basic_lg:
The pick was introduced in June 2025 in 'Chevron and Exxon Aren't Oil Giants Anymore. They're Electron Giants for the AI Age.' The argument: embodied AI generates a vast decentralized layer of always-on compute in warehouses, hospitals, homes, and factories that won't tolerate latency, making local dispatchable power essential, often in places where solar and nuclear can't scale or where grid interconnection lags. Natural gas, via modular gas-to-power, fills that gap on a timetable nuclear can't match. In August 2025 the pair trade crystallized, long energy, short software, with Chevron-versus-Salesforce performance cited as turning in energy's favor.

The most recent report (January 2026, 'The Power Constraint') deepens the moat argument around vertical integration, 'molecules to megawatts.' Chevron and Exxon can allocate 400-500 MMcf/d of gas to a single campus, build dedicated pipelines, and structure 20-year contracts bundling fuel and generation under unified accountability. With conservative assumptions of 10-20 gigawatts of new AI capacity over five years implying $70-100 billion in spend, the arbitrage is the gap between cyclical-commodity pricing and these firms' emerging role as strategic infrastructure providers.

auth.tech_deep:
The Chevron thesis is a reframe of valuation: the market prices it as a cyclical commodity producer, but Visser argues it is becoming a strategic infrastructure provider to the world's fastest-growing power consumers. The driver is the geometry of AI demand. Where the first wave was concentrated, centrally planned data centers, embodied AI spreads a decentralized 24/7 compute layer across warehouses, hospitals, homes, vehicles, and factories, none of which tolerate latency or downtime. That makes local, dispatchable power essential, frequently in regions where solar and nuclear are not feasible at scale or where grid interconnection lags, and natural gas via modular gas-to-power is the only ready, scalable, geographically flexible answer on the required timetable (nuclear deployment stays beyond 2030).

The deeper moat, developed in the January 2026 power-constraint report, is vertical integration, the ability to deliver 'molecules to megawatts' as a complete system rather than discrete components. Gigawatt-scale AI power deployment requires simultaneous orchestration of fuel logistics, generation equipment, electrical and thermal infrastructure, and controls, making multi-vendor 'plugging pieces together' operationally prohibitive. Chevron and Exxon can allocate 400-500 MMcf/d of gas to a single campus, build dedicated pipelines, and underwrite 20-year contracts bundling fuel and generation under one accountable party, with balance-sheet strength to back it.

The quantified opportunity is conservative by design: 10-20 gigawatts of new AI capacity over five years implies $70-100 billion in infrastructure spend, and the arbitrage is the gap between today's cyclical-commodity pricing and the emerging reality of multi-decade strategic-infrastructure contracts. Chevron is simultaneously the long leg of the long-energy/short-software pair, where AI surges energy demand while collapsing software margins (GPT-5's on-demand code generation threatening Salesforce, SAP, Oracle), the Chevron-versus-Salesforce divergence is his clean expression of that trade.

auth.tech_deep:
The mechanism is structural demand destruction, not cyclical weakness. Visser's core analogy: just as GLP-1 drugs make users consume less food overall rather than switch to healthier snacks, AI agents make organizations consume less software overall – they exit entire categories rather than substitute, so demand vanishes rather than shifts. Seat-based, workflow-based incumbents like Salesforce are exposed because the suppressants advance faster than organizations built to metabolize them; by the time an incumbent refactors for today's AI, tomorrow's models have already erased the need for the workflow. The GPT-5 release (August 2025) was the inflection – Schmidt and Musk noting the collapse of coding barriers lets non-programmers build apps, directly disrupting Salesforce, SAP and Oracle.

The short is also a value-trap warning aimed at a specific behavioral error: investors looking at parabolic hardware charts (Micron, SanDisk) versus abundance-era software drawdowns infer value in what has fallen – Visser says that is exactly backward. The Opus 4.5 piece reinforces it: the bottleneck is no longer access to engineering talent but domain knowledge and the ability to articulate what needs to exist, which collapses the moat of seat-priced software. Salesforce marketing its own AI platform as a headcount-reduction tool is, in his framing, the incumbent advertising its own disruption.

The trade is paired – the long leg is energy (Chevron) against the short software leg, with energy demand from AI surging while software margins face disruption. The validation is the price action: roughly a 37% decline confirmed the thesis. The risk to the call is the 'defiant' counter-positioning Visser notes – investors who insist the re-rating already happened and keep buying the dip – but he treats that defiance as confirmation, not refutation.

auth.basic_md: Teradyne appears as an unrated, second-order beneficiary of the AI semiconductor build-out rather than a discrete pick. As chip and NPU production surges, testing and tools demand gets pulled forward, with Teradyne named alongside ASML as a downstream winner. It sits within Visser's broader NPU/edge-inference thesis, where local intelligence becomes monetizable infrastructure.
auth.basic_lg:
Teradyne first appears in September 2025's 'AI Bubble or $Trillion Shift?' as a second-order beneficiary: tight DRAM supply benefits Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron, while downstream names like Teradyne and ASML see demand pulled forward as testing and tools surge. The context is Jensen Huang's view of inference demand scaling 100-1000x and China's capex surge driving exponential compute needs.

In October 2025's NPU-focused 'From the Playbook to the Play,' Teradyne is referenced within the broader thesis that NPUs turn each dollar of capex into productive cognitive capability – intelligence as monetizable, ownable infrastructure. Teradyne's role remains that of a tools/test beneficiary of the rising volume and complexity of AI silicon rather than a rated standalone call.

auth.tech_deep:
The mechanism is derived demand: as semiconductor and NPU production volumes rise, the testing and tools layer sees demand pulled forward. Visser places Teradyne alongside ASML as the clearest second-order beneficiaries, downstream of the tight-DRAM dynamic benefiting Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron. The upstream driver is Jensen Huang's projection of inference demand scaling 100-1000x and continued hyperscaler/China capex (Alibaba's 10x data-center pledge), which translates into exponential compute and therefore more chips to test.

The NPU thesis adds a structural overlay: in 'From the Playbook to the Play,' Visser argues each NPU is a capital good producing cognitive work, with intelligence becoming monetizable infrastructure as fundamental as electricity. Rising chip volume and complexity – and the move toward heterogeneous, on-device inference – sustains the test-and-tools demand that Teradyne serves.

There is no pick arc, conviction rating, or return for Teradyne in this set – it appears only as 'bullish' and 'mention' thematic references, not a rated position. The signal is that Teradyne is a pick-and-shovel proxy: leveraged to AI-silicon unit growth rather than to any single architecture winner.

auth.essence: Texas Instruments is cited as a power-semiconductor signal in the 'edge awakens' thesis, where efficient power conversion becomes the next AI scarcity trade.
auth.basic_md: Texas Instruments appears within the April 2026 'Edge Awakens' thesis that power semiconductors are the next scarcity trade as AI broadens from GPUs to the full infrastructure stack. TXN's signals (alongside Intel and NVIDIA's reported 800V DC power push in South Korea) reinforce the view that the next AI phase is defined by where intelligence happens and how efficiently it is powered. Power management becomes mission-critical across data centers, edge devices, industrial systems, and vehicles. TXN is a representative beneficiary of rising demand for efficient power conversion.
auth.basic_lg:
Texas Instruments is referenced in April 2026's power-semiconductor piece, which reframes the AI opportunity from compute-only (GPUs, memory, training) to the broader system bottleneck of power and deployment. Signals from Intel, Texas Instruments, and NVIDIA's reported 800V DC power push in South Korea are cited as reinforcing that the edge and power efficiency are converging into one investment theme.

The mechanism: every step of the AI buildout requires more efficient power conversion – data centers handling megawatt-scale racks, edge devices running intelligence locally without wasting energy, industrial systems for motors/robotics/automation, and vehicles becoming rolling compute platforms. The report stresses that the edge and data center are connected (centralized AI needs higher-voltage, higher-density systems; distributed AI needs efficient local power management), making power semiconductors a central AI beneficiary in which TXN participates.

auth.tech_deep:
The mechanism is power efficiency as a system-level bottleneck: as AI shifts from compute-dominated (GPUs, memory, training) to a broader stack spanning compute, power, and deployment, efficient power conversion becomes mission-critical at every layer. Data centers need it for megawatt-scale racks, edge devices to run intelligence locally without wasting energy, industrial systems for motors and robotics, and vehicles as they become rolling compute platforms. Texas Instruments is cited as a signal-provider in this theme, alongside Intel and NVIDIA's reported 800V DC power push in South Korea.

The bull frame is that the edge and the data center are interconnected – centralized AI demands higher-voltage, higher-density power systems while distributed AI demands efficient local power management across billions of devices – so both sides of the buildout increase power-semiconductor demand. The passages position TXN as a beneficiary rather than a deeply specified single-name call; no TXN-specific financials, bear points, or catalysts are detailed beyond its inclusion as a power-semi signal in the scarcity thesis.

pick.arc: First fully-integrated AI drug-discovery stack; GLP-1 cash funds a biological-AI moat
pick.note: Spotlight 'new pick' reiterated 5 Jun 2026 (the 'GLP-1 Manhattan Project'). Thesis-rich, modest move so far.

auth.essence: Eli Lilly is the first fully integrated AI drug-discovery stack, using GLP-1 cash flow to build a proprietary biological-intelligence moat.
auth.basic_md: Eli Lilly is a high-conviction long and Visser's boldest thematic bet: that a 150-year-old pharma giant is quietly becoming one of the most important AI companies in the world. The thesis is that Lilly is rebuilding its entire discovery pipeline around AI and using today's GLP-1 metabolic cash flow to fund a vertical, domain-specific biological-AI infrastructure that rivals can't easily replicate. The real moat is proprietary data, including decades of failed experiments, which is training data in drug discovery. The call has returned a modest ~5.4% so far (from about 1,060 to 1,117); Visser frames it as thesis-rich with the move still early.
auth.basic_lg:
The view originated in November 2025's AI drug-discovery report, which named Lilly 'the first fully integrated AI drug discovery stack', combining Insilico for small-molecule discovery, Isomorphic Labs (AlphaFold-based protein modeling), and Nvidia compute powering an 'AI drug factory.' The framing: AI shifts pharma from a labor-driven to a compute-driven model, expanding margins and setting up a historic sector re-rating, with Lilly the clearest real-world example of a giant rebuilding discovery around AI.

The thesis peaked in the June 2026 Spotlight, 'The GLP-1 Manhattan Project,' Visser's longest paper in a long time, where he ventured that by decade's end Lilly could be the largest US company and the most important AI company in the world, with 'peptides this decade equivalent to API keys from the prior decade.' The argument deepens around vertical specialization (an 'operating system for the human body'), proprietary data as the true moat, the NVIDIA co-innovation lab bridging bits and atoms via robotics and digital twins, and TuneLab/Catalyze360 as an outsourced R&D and acquisition funnel (compared to Google X). M&A is read as confirmation: GLP-1 profits buying tomorrow's biological optionality.
tech_pts: (November 2025) Named the first fully integrated AI drug-discovery stack: Insilico + Isomorphic Labs + Nvidia compute.
(November 2025) AI shifts pharma from labor-driven to compute-driven; margins expand, sector set for valuation re-rating.
(June 2026) Visser's hot takes: Lilly could be the largest US company and most important AI company by decade's end.
(June 2026) 'Peptides this decade are equivalent to API keys from the prior decade.'
(June 2026) Proprietary data is the moat; decades of failed experiments are unscrapable training data.
(June 2026) NVIDIA co-innovation lab bridges bits and atoms via robotics, digital twins, agentic wet labs.
(June 2026) TuneLab inside Catalyze360 acts as outsourced R&D network and licensing/acquisition funnel (Google X analogy).
(June 2026) Retatrutide data (cited via Bryan Johnson) likened to 'a new frontier AI model but for the body.'
(June 2026) Compounding loop: GLP-1 cash funds AI infrastructure that improves discovery and generates more data.
auth.tech_deep:
Lilly's case rests on a reframe of what kind of company it is: not a pharma stock but a vertical, domain-specific AI company building 'a new operating system for the human body.' The mechanism is a compounding loop, GLP-1 revenue funds AI infrastructure; that infrastructure improves discovery; better discovery expands the pipeline; the pipeline creates more data and revenue; the data improves the models. Unlike general AI, where the ingredients (capital, GPUs, talent, public data) are broadly available, Visser argues Lilly's race is specialized and capital-intensive in ways that cannot be built cheaply, which is precisely why GLP-1 cash flow is strategic fuel.

The core moat is data. Biology lacks the open training set the internet gave general models; the highest-value data sits inside pharma companies, clinical trials, toxicology studies, manufacturing systems, and especially failed experiments, which in drug discovery are training data. You cannot scrape decades of experimental failure, making Lilly's proprietary history defensible. The integrated stack (Insilico for small molecules, Isomorphic Labs' AlphaFold-based protein modeling, Nvidia compute) plus the NVIDIA co-innovation lab connecting AI to physical experimentation, robotics, and digital twins is what compresses biology's slow physical-world cycle time, the agentic-wet-lab and robotic-experimentation promise. TuneLab, inside Lilly Catalyze360, functions as an outsourced R&D network and a funnel for future licensing and acquisitions, a partial Google X analogy giving Lilly early sight of emerging founders, targets, and modalities.

The bull specifics are aggressive: Visser's June 2026 hot takes are that Lilly could become the largest US company and the most important AI company in the world by decade's end, with peptides as the era's 'API keys,' anchored by retatrutide data a commentator likened to a frontier AI model for the body. M&A is treated as thesis confirmation, GLP-1 profits buying biological optionality, possibly one of the highest-return uses of capital available. The honest caveat in the data is performance: despite the conviction and the thesis depth, the move has been modest (~5.4%), reflecting that the market has not yet repriced Lilly from pharma to AI infrastructure, the transition Visser says it struggles to model.

auth.essence: SanDisk is a flagged NAND/memory beneficiary of the AI 'flash wall' – a supply-constrained, scarcity-driven hardware winner with seller's-market pricing.
auth.basic_md: SanDisk appears as a scarcity-driven memory winner in the AI buildout, evidence of a supply-constrained NAND market. The standout data point is behavioral: a major NAND producer (SanDisk/Kioxia) now requires three years of prepaid cash to secure supply – more extreme than the terms TSMC imposes on NVIDIA – signaling seller's-market discipline. Visser uses SanDisk's parabolic chart (with Micron) to argue scarcity-era hardware winners deserve their re-rating, against the 'cheap' abundance-era software names.
auth.basic_lg:
SanDisk shows up in two early-2026 notes as a marker of memory scarcity. In the January 2026 'cheap software' note it is paired with Micron as a parabolic, scarcity-driven hardware winner that investors wrongly contrast with beaten-down software names like Salesforce, Adobe, and ServiceNow – Visser argues those investors 'have it exactly backward.'

The February 2026 'Flash Wall' note supplies the hard evidence: a major NAND producer (SanDisk/Kioxia) demanding three years of prepaid cash to secure supply, terms even TSMC does not require from NVIDIA. The point is that this is not ordinary semiconductor cyclicality but seller's-market discipline in a supply-constrained environment – suppliers are 'not short on cash, they are short on product.' SanDisk functions as a data point in the supply-scarcity thesis rather than a quantified single-name call.

tech_deep: SanDisk is a supporting name illustrating Visser's NAND/memory scarcity thesis. The February 2026 'Flash Wall' note centers on a behavioral signal rather than a forecast: a major NAND producer identified as SanDisk/Kioxia now requires three years of prepaid cash to secure supply, terms more extreme than even TSMC imposes on NVIDIA. The interpretation is that this is not typical semiconductor cyclicality but seller's-market discipline in a genuinely supply-constrained environment – with Phison, a primary buyer of raw NAND, forced to raise working capital materially to secure upstream allocation, and one supplier stating bluntly, 'We don't need money. We don't have any product.'

The January 2026 note frames the investment implication. SanDisk's parabolic chart, alongside Micron, is the archetype of a scarcity-driven hardware winner that investors wrongly contrast against abundance-era software drawdowns in Salesforce, Adobe, and ServiceNow. Visser's view is that buying 'cheap' fallen software is the new AI bubble trade and that investors inferring value from price declines 'have it exactly backward' – the scarcity in hardware is real and the cheapness in software is a warning, not a bargain.

There is no pick arc, return, or price target attached, so SanDisk is best read as a bullish data point within the memory-scarcity and 'flash wall' thesis rather than a standalone high-conviction call. Its weight comes from the prepayment signal – a buyer-side behavior that Visser argues carries more weight than any seller talking up inventory.

pick.arc: Inelastic byproduct supply meets AI/solar/defense demand — interface metal at every failure point
pick.note: Thesis opened late Jan 2026; the only commodity in his 100-name compute portfolio. Up sharply on the year, though it gave back ~9% in the month into mid-Jun.

auth.essence: Silver is Visser's high-conviction critical mineral for AI – the cheap, irreplaceable interface metal at every electrical failure point, meeting inelastic byproduct supply.
auth.basic_md: Silver is the only commodity in Visser's 100-name compute portfolio and a high-conviction long opened in late January 2026. The thesis: silver is the 'interface material' at the electrical failure points of AI systems – contacts, switches, connectors, solders, power electronics, photovoltaics – so its demand rises as systems scale, even though it is a tiny share of any bill of materials. Because it is cheap but functionally critical, demand is price-inelastic; layered onto persistent structural supply deficits and inelastic byproduct supply, that sets up a repricing. It is up sharply on the year (about +44%).
auth.basic_lg:
The thesis opened with the January 2026 'Why AI Is Running Into Silver' note, which positions silver as the electrical analog to high-bandwidth memory: just as HBM became the choke point between compute and its utilization, silver sits at the 'narrow waist' of electrical systems where electrons pass through small spaces under high current and reliability demands. The key insight is that in exponential systems, failures at interfaces cascade rather than average out, so silver demand rises with scale.

The April 2026 deep-dive ('Silver: A Critical Mineral in the Efficient Transfer of Energy Into Compute') hardens the case: silver is economically important precisely because it is cheap, functionally critical, and hard to economize away. The inference transition (tokens per watt, whole-system efficiency) broadens demand, edge AI extends it, and defense adds a multiplier – Ukraine planned ~4.5 million FPV drones in 2025 (up from 1.5 million in 2024), each carrying small but multiplied silver content. Visser stresses that most investors still misread silver as merely part of the gold trade, which is where the opportunity lies; the April 2026 note flags persistent structural deficits as the supply backdrop.
tech_pts: (January 2026) The only commodity in his 100-name thematic compute portfolio
(January 2026) Positioned as the electrical analog to high-bandwidth memory – the interface that gates power infrastructure
(January 2026) Sits at electrical 'failure points': contacts, switches, connectors, solders, conductive pastes, power electronics, PV metallization
(April 2026) Demand is price-inelastic because silver is a tiny share of hyperscale data-center cost (land, power, transformers, cooling, GPUs dominate)
(April 2026) Inference transition (tokens per watt, cost per token, thermal efficiency) strengthens the thesis
(April 2026) Defense multiplier: Ukraine planned ~4.5M FPV drones in 2025 vs 1.5M in 2024; capacity ~4M/year
(April 2026) Market running persistent structural deficits; 'silver is the performance metal of embodied AI' while copper is the volume metal
(April 2026) Most investors misread silver as part of the gold trade – the perception gap is the opportunity
(pick) Up ~44% on the year, though it gave back ~9% in the month into mid-June 2026
tech_deep: Visser's silver thesis reframes a precious metal as a critical AI input. The January 2026 note builds the analogy to memory: just as compute without high-bandwidth memory stalls – arithmetic units idling while waiting for data – power infrastructure cannot keep pace with intelligence without silver at the interface. Silver appears at the 'narrow waist' of electrical systems, in contacts, switches, connectors, solders, conductive pastes, power electronics, and photovoltaic metallization. These are failure points, and in exponential systems failures at interfaces cascade rather than average out, so silver demand rises as systems scale.

The April 2026 deep-dive develops the economic mechanism: in a hyperscale deployment the large cost buckets are land, power, transformers, cooling, GPUs, memory, and networking, so silver is only a tiny share of the bill. That is precisely why demand is price-inelastic – a more expensive relay or thermal interface won't cancel a server deployment; the system absorbs it. The inference transition reinforces the case as the industry optimizes tokens per watt, cost per token, and whole-system electrical and thermal efficiency, and edge AI pushes the same logic further. Visser positions silver as the 'performance metal of embodied AI' against copper as the 'volume metal' of electrification.

Two amplifiers and one supply argument complete the case. Defense is a demand multiplier: with Ukraine planning roughly 4.5 million FPV drones in 2025 (up from over 1.5 million in 2024) and capacity near 4 million annually, even small per-unit silver content multiplies meaningfully, though Visser concedes the exact per-drone content is undisclosed. Crucially, silver enters this wave from structural deficit – the market has run persistent shortfalls – meaning AI infrastructure, defense, and solar demand collide with inelastic byproduct supply. The standing inefficiency is perceptual: most investors still treat silver as part of the gold trade, and that misreading is the opportunity. The trade is up about 44% on the year, with a roughly 9% give-back into mid-June 2026.

auth.essence: Coherent appears as an optical-networking name inside Visser's AI supply-chain fragility thesis, where bandwidth and optics are an emerging chokepoint.
auth.basic_md: Mentioned, not picked, in two reports framed around AI supply-chain bottlenecks. Coherent sits in the optical-networking layer that Visser flags as a tightening chokepoint as the AI buildout scrambles for the same finite resources – HBM, advanced packaging, power, cooling, and optics. The May 2026 "Bandwidth Wars" piece uses Fujikura's post-earnings collapse as the first real glimpse of demand outrunning the physical supply chain. No discrete conviction or direction is attached.
auth.basic_lg:
Coherent's context is built across two 2026 reports about the physical limits of the AI buildout. The March 2026 "OpenClaw" piece argues the next AI phase is decided by who can move data through memory, power, and latency constraints – framing optical networking and packaging as the binding layers and citing Meta's up-to-$6-billion Corning deal to secure optical fiber as evidence of the networking wall.

The most recent May 2026 "Bandwidth Wars" report makes the supply-chain fragility explicit: the AI buildout is resembling the Strait of Hormuz – a narrow choke point through which urgent global demand tries to pass simultaneously across HBM, advanced packaging, power, cooling, optical networking, and chemicals. Fujikura's share collapse despite extraordinary results is cited as the market's first real glimpse of that risk in practice, placing Coherent's optical-networking exposure squarely in the fragility frame.
tech_pts: (May 2026) Optical networking named among finite AI supply chains (HBM, packaging, power, cooling, chemicals) being scrambled for simultaneously.
(May 2026) AI buildout likened to the Strait of Hormuz: a narrow choke point absorbing urgent simultaneous global demand.
(May 2026) Fujikura (5803) shares collapsed after earnings despite extraordinary results – first glimpse of demand outrunning supply.
(March 2026) Next AI phase decided by who moves data through memory, power, and latency constraints most efficiently.
(March 2026) Meta's up-to-$6 billion Corning deal to secure optical fiber exclusively highlights the networking wall.
auth.tech_deep:
Coherent is not given a standalone thesis; it appears as an optical-networking name within Visser's broader argument that the binding near-term risk to the AI capex boom has flipped from weak demand to supply that cannot keep pace. The mechanism: realizing the economics of the heterogeneous AI factory requires moving data efficiently through memory, power, and latency constraints, and copper plus insufficient bandwidth becomes the wall – pushing value toward optical networking. Meta's up-to-$6-billion agreement with Corning to secure optical fiber exclusively for its AI data centers is cited as a facility-scale signal of that networking wall.

The most recent framing is one of fragility rather than pure upside. Visser likens the AI buildout to the Strait of Hormuz – a narrow choke point through which urgent global demand is trying to pass simultaneously across HBM, advanced packaging, power equipment, cooling, optical networking, and chemicals – and points to Fujikura's post-earnings share collapse despite extraordinary reported results as the market's first real glimpse of what that synchronized-scramble risk looks like in practice. The implication for optical names like Coherent is double-edged: they sit at a genuine chokepoint of structural demand, but the same dynamic carries the risk that even strong results disappoint when expectations and supply constraints collide. No price, direction, or conviction level is assigned.

auth.basic_md: Entegris is Jordi Visser's spotlight 'new pick' built on the 'missing chemical layer of the AI economy' – the high-purity chemicals, filtration, CMP, deposition and contamination-control materials that make AI chips manufacturable. The key insight is a shift from a unit-growth story (how many wafers) to a materials-intensity story (how much complexity per wafer): more HBM, deeper NAND layers, advanced logic, and advanced packaging each raise Entegris content per wafer. The market's likely mistake is treating it as a cyclical consumables name tied to capex and wafer starts rather than a structural AI-complexity beneficiary. Picked April 2026, added to after Vera Rubin confirmed the chemical-layer story in June 2026; return ~6.7% so far (anchor ~150 to ~160), still early.
auth.basic_lg:
The thesis launched in the April 2026 SPOTLIGHT report. Jensen Huang's five-layer cake (energy, chips, infrastructure, models, applications) is used to argue that high-purity chemicals and materials science are a missing infrastructure layer running through four of those five categories. Entegris sits in that problem set with CMP slurries and pads, deposition materials, etch and clean chemistries, filtration and purification, fluid-handling, FOUPs and wafer-handling. The original framing: every additional process step is a fresh chance for contamination to destroy value, so as chips grow more complex Entegris sells more of what makes that complexity manufacturable. Management's Q4 2025 commentary crystallized the inflection – 2025 unit-driven growth in liquid filtration, selective etch and CMP, with CEO Reeder explicitly tying adoption of more complex device architectures (to support AI) to higher content per wafer. The CHIPS Act award is cited as validating a domestic materials supply chain; HBM4 and advanced packaging are the process-intensity multiplier that makes Entegris a 'purity tax' on every successful wafer start.

The June 2026 update ('Vera Rubin Confirms the Missing Chemical Layer') reinforces rather than changes the thesis. Vera Rubin's rack-scale integration – compute, memory, networking, storage and packaging designed together – gives a more specific map of where complexity is going, and every design choice raises materials-science importance. A notable new wrinkle: AI is pulling NAND into the performance architecture (less like cheap external storage, more like part of the AI memory hierarchy), and NAND layer scaling is one of the cleanest examples of content-per-wafer growth (more etch, deposition, cleans, CMP, filtration). The long-tail signal is orbital/space-based data centers – not a near-term revenue driver, but a marker that compute is hitting physical limits, and a specialty-materials problem (radiation hardening, hermetic packaging, novel thermal). The setup: if wafer starts rise Entegris wins, and if content per wafer rises it wins more.

The core mechanism is content-per-wafer growth driven by AI manufacturing complexity. Every step in fabricating advanced AI chips – etch, deposition, cleans, CMP, filtration, contamination control – is a point where impurity destroys yield, so as device architectures grow more complex (HBM4, hybrid bonding, more advanced logic nodes, advanced packaging), Entegris sells more of the consumables and materials that make complexity manufacturable. The reframe is decisive: the old semiconductor question ('how many wafers are being started?') gives way to the AI-era question ('how much complexity is embedded in each wafer?'). Entegris is thus positioned as a 'purity tax' on every successful wafer start, mapped via Jensen Huang's five-layer cake as the missing chemical infrastructure layer underneath energy, chips, infrastructure and applications.

The bull specifics tighten across the two reports. Q4 2025 commentary showed unit-driven growth in liquid filtration, selective etch and CMP concentrated in the most advanced technologies, with CEO Reeder explicitly tying customers' adoption of more complex AI device architectures to higher content per wafer – the desired progression from 'purity matters' to 'AI complexity is driving higher content.' The CHIPS Act award validates a strategic domestic materials supply chain. June 2026's Vera Rubin update adds rack-scale integration (compute/memory/networking/storage/packaging designed together) as a complexity map, and a fresh vector – AI pulling NAND from cheap external storage into the performance memory hierarchy, where NAND layer scaling is the cleanest content-per-wafer driver (more etch, deposition, cleans, CMP, filtration). Orbital/space-based data centers are flagged as a long-tail specialty-materials signal (radiation hardening, hermetic packaging, novel thermal), not near-term revenue.

The key risk is precisely the market's framing: Entegris is a structurally PMI-sensitive material reflecting real-time manufacturing-cycle velocity, and the consensus mistake is to treat it as a cyclical consumables name tied to capex and wafer starts rather than a structural AI-complexity beneficiary. The pick is still early (added June 2026 after Vera Rubin confirmed the chemical-layer story), with a modest ~6.7% return from a ~150 anchor to ~160. Catalysts are content-multiplying transitions – HBM4 and advanced packaging ramps, NAND layer-count increases, migration to more advanced logic nodes – any of which raises content per wafer regardless of whether unit wafer starts also rise.
